attributes.stem:
A 55-year-old man presents with a 12 month history of progressive difficulty in swallowing. He has increasing problems with solids but not liquids. His medical history includes gastro-oesophageal reflux disease for which he took omeprazole. This resolved his symptoms and he stopped taking the medication about six years ago. He has not had any heartburn symptoms over the last two years. He has lost about 3 kg weight during this time. He has a 30 pack-year smoking history and drinks about three cans of beer a day.

On examination his blood pressure is 150/90 mmHg, pulse 94/min and temperature 37.2C. His BMI is 30 kg/m2. The rest of the physical examination is unremarkable. A barium swallow is performed and the result is shown (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Squamous cell carcinoma of the oesophagus
attributes.explanation:
The contrast study shows an area of concentric narrowing in the mid-oesophagus above a hiatus hernia with hold-up of contrast above the stricture. There are no irregularities to the strictured area. Taken together with the history of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD), the cessation of reflux symptoms and the radiological findings, the diagnosis of a peptic oesophageal stricture is very likely. Prior to the advent of the proton pump inhibitors, peptic strictures were a relatively common complication of chronic GORD, but now are infrequently seen. Characteristically, these strictures present with progressive dysphagia to solids. As the stricture progresses, acid reflux into the oesophagus lessens and the heartburn symptoms disappear. Even though the clinical and radiological picture fits for a benign peptic stricture, an upper endoscopy is essential to rule out malignancy. The first-line treatment for these peptic strictures is endoscopic dilatation.

Achalasia is an oesophageal motility disorder that causes dysphagia with both solids and liquids. Barium swallow in such cases would reveal dilatation of the proximal oesophagus and “bird beak” narrowing in the distal oesophagus. Patients with achalasia have regurgitation, but not reflux symptoms.

Diffuse oesophageal spasm is a motility disorder characterised by uncoordinated non-peristaltic oesophageal contractions. It presents with intermittent dysphagia and chest pain. A barium swallow may reveal a “corkscrew appearance” of the oesophagus.

Most cases of oesophageal adenocarcinoma arise from Barrett’s oesophagus which is another complication of GORD. Although patients with GORD are at risk of adenocarcinoma, this patient is unlikely to have adenocarcinoma as it is unlikely that the patient would have tolerated his symptoms for 12 months. In addition, the contrast study would be more likely to show an irregular strictured area.

Smoking is a risk factor for oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma and these tumours are found more often in the upper and mid thirds of the oesophagus. This patient has an involvement of the distal oesophagus. Oesophageal malignancy is usually associated with rapid, substantial weight loss and progressive dysphagia.
learningPoints: With the efficacy of current medications for controlling gastro-oesophageal reflux, peptic oesophageal strictures are now uncommon complications.
references: Debi U, Sharma M, Singh L, Sinha A. Barium esophagogram in various esophageal diseases: A pictorial essay [Internet]. The Indian journal of radiology & imaging. Wolters Kluwer - Medknow; 2019 [cited 2021Jan22]. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6639862/
